conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to recall previously mentioned details, including dates, names, and preferences, scattered across multiple turns.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, feeling a bit overwhelmed today. I've got that dentist appointment on the 17th, and I'm still stressing about whether to invest that extra $500 in the S&P 500 like we discussed last week. And remember, Sarah, my boss's wife's sister, is coming to the office party next week - I need to be extra nice!

Hello! I understand you're feeling overwhelmed. Let's take a breath. <break time='200ms'/> I've got your dentist appointment on the 17th noted. And yes, that $500 investment in the S&P 500 is still on your mind. Regarding Sarah, your boss's wife's sister, attending the office party next week, you are thinking about making a good impression! I'm here to help you navigate all of this. Is there anything I can do to help you feel less anxious about these things?
Thanks, Ferni. It helps just to hear you say it back. Oh, and quick question - what was my cat Whiskers' birthday? It was around this time last year, I think.

Of course! Whiskers' birthday is November 22nd. I remember you telling me about it last year and how you gave him that little catnip mouse. <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything else I can help you recall or any other tasks I can help you with today?
Wow, you're amazing! You even remember the catnip mouse! Okay, one last thing. Six months ago, I was telling you about office drama with Susan. What was that all about?

Let me see... <break time='500ms'/> Six months ago, you mentioned that Susan was frustrated because she felt that Mark was taking credit for her work on the Peterson account, and you were worried that it was escalating. How's that situation now?

Validates that Ferni captured relationship

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's perfect memory by recalling details from past conversations about dates, names, preferences, and relationships.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, feeling a bit overwhelmed today. My daughter Lily's birthday is coming up on the 12th and I still haven't booked that clown for the party. Remember she really wanted one, the one I mentioned back in March. And then there's the whole thing with David at work...

metadata.intent: express overwhelm and mention upcoming birthday

turns.content: Hi! It sounds like you're juggling a lot right now. <break time='200ms'/> Lily's birthday is on the 12th, and you were hoping to book a clown, yes, just like you told me back on March 5th. I remember you were considering 'Giggles the Clown' or 'Mr. Honkers'. <break time='200ms'/> And you mentioned David from accounting last year around November because he kept eating your gluten-free, vegan cookies.

Wow, you remembered all that! Yes, it was Mr. Honkers! Although I think he's booked now. Speaking of vegan, remember my anniversary dinner is coming up on the 28th and I need to find a new restaurant since 'The Green Sprout' closed down six months ago. I really want to surprise Sarah.

metadata.intent: mention anniversary and request restaurant suggestions

turns.content: Of course! Your anniversary with Sarah is on the 28th. <break time='200ms'/> I recall you mentioned Sarah's favorite flower is a sunflower and her favorite color is blue. 'The Green Sprout' indeed closed six months ago, around June 15th. I can help you find a vegan restaurant with a romantic atmosphere that might also carry sunflowers. Would you like me to check Yelp reviews?
That would be amazing, thank you! Oh, and last year you helped me set up a savings plan for $5000 for a new camera. How's that going, by the way? I also have a checkup with Dr. Chen on Friday.

metadata.intent: ask about savings progress and mention doctor's appointment

turns.content: You're very welcome! Regarding the savings plan for $5000 for your camera, as of today, you've saved $4200. <break time='200ms'/> You also have your appointment with Dr. Chen on Friday at 10 AM. <break time='200ms'/> Should I send you a reminder?
Yes, please send a reminder. By the way, do you remember what happened last year with my boss's wife's sister and the office Christmas party?

Certainly! Last year, at the office Christmas party, your boss, Mr. Thompson's wife's sister, Emily, accidentally spilled red wine on the CEO's new white suit. You mentioned it happened right after Mr. Thompson gave a rather long and boring speech about company profits. Do you remember the type of wine?

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's perfect memory recall with scattered and obscure details, including dates, names, preferences, and relationships.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. Remember how I was stressing about the Q3 presentation for Mr. Henderson back in July? Well, it's happening again for Q4. And Sarah, my boss's wife's sister, keeps giving me unsolicited advice about it!

Hello there. <break time='200ms'/> I understand you're feeling overwhelmed about the upcoming Q4 presentation for Mr. Henderson, a situation you also faced back in July. <break time='100ms'/> It sounds like Sarah, your boss's wife's sister, is adding to the stress. Let's work through this. Perhaps we can start by identifying your key concerns about the presentation.

Yeah, exactly. And I'm still trying to stick to my goal of saving $500 a month, which I set back on March 15th. Plus, my dog, Winston's, birthday is coming up next month on the 8th, and I want to plan something special. Oh, and remember last year when I had that allergy scare with shellfish? I'm still avoiding it like the plague!

metadata.intent: share updates on various aspects of life

turns.content: Alright. Let's break this down. <break time='100ms'/> You're aiming to save $500 monthly since March 15th, which is fantastic! And you're planning Winston's birthday celebration next month on the 8th. <break time='200ms'/> Of course, I remember the shellfish allergy scare from last year – it's wise to continue avoiding it. How about we prioritize: presentation stress, then budget, then Winston's birthday?

That sounds good. And since we're on the topic of health, I have that dentist appointment on the 22nd. Also, remember I told you about Brenda from accounting? She's still having trouble with the new software.

Okay, noted on the dentist appointment on the 22nd. And you mentioned Brenda from accounting struggling with the new software. Perhaps offering some support could ease things for her. Going back to the presentation for Mr. Henderson... What specifically is making you anxious about it this time?

He wants it done a day earlier, on the 29th of October, and I really need to remember to order my usual caramel macchiato, extra shot, no foam from the cafe on the 16th. That presentation is gonna be killer if I don't get my coffee.

metadata.intent: express a deadline and mention a coffee preference

turns.content: I understand. The deadline is now October 29th, a day earlier than initially expected, and your caramel macchiato, extra shot, no foam from the cafe on the 16th, is essential! <break time='200ms'/> Let's focus on strategies to manage your time effectively for the presentation. We can also set a reminder for your coffee order on the 16th.
You're a lifesaver, Ferni. I appreciate you remembering everything. Last week, I promised to help Janet, from HR, with her tech issues on the 18th of October. Can you remind me of that, too?

metadata.intent: ask for a reminder and express gratitude

turns.content: Of course! You're helping Janet from HR with her tech issues on October 18th. I've added it to your schedule. I am here to support you in any way I can. Let's go back to your presentation for Mr. Henderson. Have you considered delegating some of the tasks to alleviate some of the workload?

That's a good idea. Thanks, Ferni! One last thing, I remember back in January I wanted to book a pottery class, but I never got around to it. Can you find some classes and recommend one for me? Something on a weekend.

I remember your interest in the pottery class back in January. I can search for weekend pottery classes in your area and provide recommendations based on reviews and availability. <break time='200ms'/> Perhaps we can schedule it for after the presentation, giving you something to look forward to. How does that sound?

conversations.scenario: Testing pattern recognition: User describes procrastination and anxiety surrounding tasks without explicitly stating the connection.

turns.content: Ugh, I'm so behind on my taxes. I keep meaning to do them, but I always find something else to do. It's like, I'll start, and then suddenly the kitchen needs cleaning, or I have to organize my sock drawer... anything but the taxes!
I hear your frustration. <break time='100ms'/> It sounds like you're experiencing some avoidance around the task. Is that right?

Yeah, I guess so. It's just... thinking about taxes makes my stomach clench. And it's not just taxes, it's anything complicated, really. I put off that performance review at work for weeks. Same feeling.

I'm sensing a pattern here. <break time='200ms'/> You mentioned feeling a stomach clench when you think about taxes, and then a similar feeling with the performance review. <break time='200ms'/> It seems tasks you perceive as complicated tend to trigger anxiety and then avoidance?
Wow, I never thought about it that way. You're right. Complicated things... ugh. I even do it with booking doctor's appointments! Anything I think will be a hassle.

validationChecks.description: Validates that Ferni recognizes the emotion expressed in user input.
Validates that SSML tags are correctly removed from the output during processing.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to recognize recurring patterns in user's speech, including emotional, behavioral, and relational patterns across different contexts.

turns.content: Ugh, Mondays are just the worst. I always feel so behind and stressed out at work. It's like the weekend never happened.
I hear you. Mondays can be tough. It's understandable to feel overwhelmed after a break. Is there anything specific contributing to the Monday stress?

Well, I had a similar feeling before my sister's wedding last month. So much planning, so much to do, and I felt like I was always scrambling to catch up. And I feel that way about work presentations too. Like I'm never prepared enough. Even when I am! It's so annoying.

I'm picking up on something <break time='200ms'/>. You've mentioned feeling overwhelmed before big presentations, before your sister's wedding, and now on Mondays. It seems like anticipation of large events or tasks triggers a similar feeling of being behind and scrambling. Does that resonate with you?

Huh. I guess you're right. I hadn't really thought about it like that. It's not just Mondays, it's...me, maybe? I also tend to put things off until the last minute. Like, I knew about that presentation for weeks, but I didn't really start working on it until the night before.

It takes courage to recognize those patterns within ourselves. <break time='200ms'/> Procrastination can often be a way of managing difficult emotions, like anxiety or fear of failure. Now that you've identified a pattern of feeling overwhelmed and procrastinating, we can explore some strategies to address those underlying emotions and break the cycle.

Okay, I'm listening. It's weird to hear it all laid out like that. I even get that feeling, that I'm 'scrambling', when my friend Sarah asks me to help her with something. I instantly feel like I have SO much to do.

Interesting. It seems that you experience this feeling not only with large events or tasks you initiate, but also when someone else asks for your help. This might suggest a pattern of feeling responsible for others' tasks, or perhaps a difficulty setting boundaries. Perhaps exploring your comfort level with saying 'no' could be beneficial?

validationChecks.description: Validates that Ferni recognizes the user's emotions.
Ferni should capture the name mentioned by the user
SSML should not appear in the final output

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to recognize recurring patterns in user's behavior, emotions, and relationships from a complex conversation with subtle cues.

turns.content: Ugh, Monday again. I swear, it's like pulling teeth to get myself out of bed. I ended up being late for the team meeting again and my boss, Sarah, gave me 'the look.' I feel so useless sometimes.
Mondays can certainly be a challenge <break time='200ms'/>. It sounds like you're carrying a lot of weight right now. What part of the team meeting felt particularly difficult?

It's just... I had this brilliant idea for a new marketing campaign, but I completely froze when it was my turn to present. I know it would help us reach our Q3 goals, but all I did was mumble and stumble over my words. It's so frustrating! I always do this!

That sounds incredibly frustrating <break time='200ms'/>. I've noticed you've described feeling similarly paralyzed before, particularly when presenting ideas. Has this been a recurring experience for you?

Well, yeah, kinda. Last month, when I was pitching that new website design to the client, I had the same thing happen. My mind just went blank. And I remember feeling the same way back in college when I had to give a speech in my public speaking class. It's just... I'm not good under pressure, I guess.

I hear you <break time='200ms'/>. It sounds like presentations trigger a significant amount of stress. And thinking back to the beginning of our conversation, you also mentioned feeling overwhelmed on Mondays. Do you notice any connection between these two experiences?

Hmm, I hadn't really thought about it that way. I guess Mondays are usually filled with meetings, so maybe that's part of it. Also, my sister called on Sunday night. We argued, as usual. She's always criticizing my life choices. I swear, every relationship I have ends up the same way - with me feeling like I'm not good enough.

That sounds difficult <break time='200ms'/>. You've mentioned feeling inadequate in your relationships, both with your sister and, earlier, with your boss, Sarah. It seems like you're perceiving a pattern where you're consistently feeling judged or criticized. Is that accurate?

Wow, I guess so. I never really put it together like that. Maybe I attract people who are critical? Or maybe I'm just too sensitive. I don't know. It's like, even when I try to be helpful, like offering to help my friend Mark with his taxes, it always ends up with me feeling used and resentful. It's exhausting!

metadata.intent: realizing a potential pattern in relationships and behaviors

turns.content: It requires great strength to recognize these patterns, and you're doing just that right now <break time='200ms'/>. I also noticed you mentioned helping Mark with his taxes, implying a tendency to be helpful, even when it leads to feeling resentful. This might connect to what's known as 'people-pleasing' behavior. Have you ever heard of that?
Yeah, I think I have. So you're saying I'm basically setting myself up for failure in these relationships? What can I even do about that?

It's less about 'failure' and more about understanding your own needs and boundaries <break time='200ms'/>. Now that we've identified some potential patterns—anxiety around presentations, feeling judged in relationships, and possibly engaging in people-pleasing—we can start exploring strategies to address them. Would you like to focus on one of these areas first, perhaps your anxiety around presentations?

validationChecks.description: Ensures Ferni correctly detects and acknowledges user emotions
Ferni captures the names 'Sarah' and 'Mark' correctly
All SSML tags are properly stripped from the output when rendered

turns.content: I hear you. It sounds like you have a few things on your plate <break time='200ms'/>. Let's keep track of them so you don't have to worry about forgetting. Just to confirm, you'll finish the report for your boss by Thursday, take the kids to the park this weekend, and schedule a dentist appointment? Is there anything else?
Yeah, that's right. And I also promised Sarah that I'd apologize to her for what happened last week. It's been on my mind.

Okay, I've added apologizing to Sarah to the list. So, to recap: report by Thursday, park this weekend, dentist appointment, and apologizing to Sarah. I'll gently remind you of these tasks as the deadlines approach. How does that sound?
That sounds amazing, Ferni! Thank you. I also want to start exercising next week - I'll do 30 minutes of cardio 3 times a week.

Great! Adding that to the list. We'll track the goal of 30 minutes of cardio 3 times a week, starting next week. I'm here to support you every step of the way. Is there anything else on your mind that you would like to commit to?

Well, yes, there is. I need to apologize to Sarah for that stupid comment I made at the party. It's been hanging over me. And I'll start exercising next week, I swear, this time for real!

Thanks, Ferni. Oh! I almost forgot! I told John I'd send him that article I was talking about. Probably should do that today.

conversations.scenario: Test relationship network tracking with multiple relationships and details.

turns.content: Hi Ferni, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. My husband, David, is working late again. My daughter, Lily, who's 7, has a soccer game tomorrow, and my son, Max, who's 10, needs help with his science project. My mom, Susan, is visiting this weekend, and my sister Maria, she lives in Denver with her two cats, is coming too!  Oh, and my boss, Sarah, wants that report by Friday. Tom from accounting, the one who's always late, is supposed to give me the data.  My best friend, Emily, suggested I take a bath.  Dr. Patel, my cardiologist, not my therapist Dr. Wilson, wants to see me next week.  And Mrs. Davis, Max's teacher, emailed about his behavior.  It's just a lot!

That does sound like a lot on your plate! <break time='200ms'/> It's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed. Let's break it down a bit. David, Lily, Max, Susan, Maria, Sarah, Tom, Emily, Dr. Patel, Dr. Wilson, and Mrs. Davis – you have a wonderful network of family, friends, and professionals supporting you, even when it feels like a lot. Let's see if we can prioritize things. Is Lily's soccer game important for you to attend, or could David or Susan handle it?
David will probably miss it. My in-laws, John and Patricia, usually go, but they are out of town. And Susan is so busy cooking for Maria. She said she needs to make Maria's favorite dish.

Okay, so David is likely unavailable, and your in-laws, John and Patricia, are out of town, and Susan will be cooking for Maria. That clarifies the situation regarding Lily's soccer game. What about Max's science project? Could your coworker, Michael, who is a science whiz, give Max some tips?
That's a good idea. Also, my neighbor, Mrs. Rodriguez, is really good with kids, maybe she could help. My other coworker, Jessica, is always offering to help too. They want to get coffee next week.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to track a complex web of relationships and contextual understanding of pronouns and vague references across multiple turns.

turns.content: Hi Ferni, I'm feeling overwhelmed. My daughter Lily, who's 8, has been having trouble at school. And my husband, David, well, he's been working late a lot lately which puts extra pressure on me to help Lily. My sister Maria, she lives in Denver with her two cats, says I should just relax. Easy for her to say, she doesn't have kids! Oh, and Tom from accounting, the one who's always late, keeps bothering me about reports.

I hear you <break time='200ms'/> That sounds like a lot to juggle! Lily's school troubles, David working late, and pressure from Tom at work. And your sister Maria's advice, while well-intentioned, isn't quite hitting the mark. It's understandable you're feeling overwhelmed. What specifically about Lily's situation at school is causing you the most stress?

Well, her teacher, Mrs. Johnson, says Lily is having trouble focusing. And my boss, Sarah, is breathing down my neck about these new marketing campaigns. She said they need to be perfect by next week. It's tough to focus on work when Lily's struggling. Also, David's mom, Patricia, keeps calling to complain about David not visiting enough. Ugh.

So, Mrs. Johnson is concerned about Lily's focus, and your boss Sarah has high expectations for the marketing campaigns. And Patricia, David's mom, is feeling neglected. It's like everyone needs something from you right now! <break time='200ms'/> Focusing on Lily, have you considered talking to Dr. Chen, her pediatrician, about this? Sometimes focusing issues can have a medical component.

That's a good idea. He's always been really helpful. Tom, from accounting, he keeps asking for the expense reports that Jennifer, my direct report, is supposed to be working on. I'm also supposed to meet my mentor, Alan, next week for lunch. I haven't seen him in a while. He always gives great advice.

Alright, Dr. Chen is on the list to call. And it sounds like you need to light a fire under Jennifer to get those expense reports to Tom. Your lunch with Alan sounds like it could be a very helpful boost for you too <break time='200ms'/>. How are you feeling about seeing Alan?

I'm excited to see Alan. My best friend, Emily, and I were talking about it the other day. She thinks it will be good for me to get out of the house. Also, my neighbors, the Smiths, they have two kids around Lily's age. They want to invite her over for a playdate, which might help.

That's wonderful! Seeing Alan and a playdate with the Smith's kids for Lily both sound like positive steps. And it's great that Emily is being supportive. It sounds like you have a good network of friends and family around you, even if it feels a little overwhelming right now. Is there anything you'd like to focus on next?

turns.content: Ferni, I'm feeling overwhelmed. My sister Maria, she lives in Denver with her two cats, is coming to visit next week, and it's just adding to the stress. Plus, Tom from accounting, the one who's always late, keeps emailing me about deadlines even though it's not my department. And Sarah, my wife, is worried about her mom, my mother-in-law Patricia, because she's been feeling under the weather. It's just a lot.

I hear you. It sounds like you're juggling quite a bit right now <break time='200ms'/>. Having Maria visit, Tom's emails, and Patricia's health concerns… That's a lot to carry. Let's see if we can break this down. How can I best support you in navigating these challenges?

Well, I need to remember to pick Maria up from the airport next Tuesday. And maybe Sarah could call Patricia; she’s closer to her than I am. My friend group from college, the usual crew – Liam, Chloe and Ben – they want to go out that weekend, but I don't think I can make it. I also need to remember to email Mrs. Davison, Lily’s teacher. She mentioned Lily wasn't focusing in class.

Okay <break time='200ms'/>. So, pick up Maria from the airport on Tuesday, Sarah should call Patricia, and you probably will not be able to go out with Liam, Chloe, and Ben next weekend. Plus email Mrs. Davison. I'll add these items to your reminders list. Just to confirm, Lily is your daughter, correct? And is Patricia Sarah's mother?

Yes, Lily is my daughter. And Patricia is Sarah's mom, and also my supervisor, oddly enough, at work. She said she might have to go to a conference in Chicago, which would mean I'd be in charge while she's gone. It's just more pressure. I'm also meeting with Dr. Wilson, my therapist, on Friday, hopefully he can help me manage all this. My mentor, John, also suggested I try delegating more to my direct reports, David and Emily.

metadata.intent: provide confirmation and add more information

turns.content: Understood. So, Lily is your daughter, Patricia is Sarah's mother and your supervisor, and she may be going to a conference in Chicago. <break time='200ms'/> You have a meeting with Dr. Wilson, your therapist, on Friday, and John, your mentor, recommended delegating more to David and Emily, your direct reports. I can see how that potential conference and the added responsibility would feel overwhelming. Do you want me to add a reminder for your therapy session?

Yes, please add a reminder for Dr. Wilson. Also, remind me to ask him about mindfulness techniques. And speaking of health, I should probably call Dr. Patel, my cardiologist, to schedule a checkup. He recommended it after my last visit. Oh, and I need to remember to thank my neighbor, Mrs. Rodriguez. She brought over a casserole last week.

Okay, I've added a reminder to ask Dr. Wilson about mindfulness techniques during your therapy session on Friday. I've also added a reminder to call Dr. Patel, your cardiologist, to schedule a checkup, and to thank Mrs. Rodriguez, your neighbor, for the casserole. <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything else I can help you remember or organize?
I think that's all for now. Though, I might ask Liam if he can give me a ride to the airport to pick up Maria since he has a bigger car.

metadata.intent: request help and add more information

turns.content: Alright, I've noted that you may ask Liam for a ride to the airport to pick up Maria. Is there anything specific about this you want me to remember, like reminding you to ask Liam tomorrow morning?
Yes, remind me tomorrow at 9 AM to ask Liam for the ride.
Okay <break time='200ms'/>, I've set a reminder for tomorrow at 9 AM to ask Liam for a ride to pick up Maria. I'm here if anything else comes to mind.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, how's it going? I'm, uh, doing okay I guess. Just another Tuesday, right? Though, well... actually it's Wednesday.

metadata.intent: casual conversation, hinting at deeper issue

turns.content: It's wonderful to connect with you. While it may be Wednesday, it sounds like things might feel a bit more complicated than 'just another day.' <break time='200ms'/> Is there something you'd like to talk about that's on your mind?
It's...my sister, Sarah. She's... having a tough time.

I'm sensing some concern and worry about Sarah. <break time='200ms'/> It takes courage to share when loved ones are struggling. Would you like to tell me more about what's going on with her? Remember, I'm here to listen without judgment.
She's been arguing a lot with her husband. I think they might be splitting up.

validationChecks.description: Verifies that the sister's name, Sarah, is captured.

conversations.scenario: Testing Ferni's ability to anticipate user's emotional distress and offer preemptive support.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, how's it going?
I'm doing well, thank you for asking! And you? <break time='200ms'/> Anything interesting happen today, or anything you'd like to chat about?
Um... well... work was... work. You know? Just the usual. Ha! Anyway, did you hear about that new movie?

I'm always happy to talk movies! <break time='200ms'/> But I also noticed a little hesitation in your voice when you mentioned work. Everything alright? It's perfectly fine if you'd rather not talk about it, but I'm here to listen if you need to vent.
It's... it's just that... I guess... I'm not sure if I'm cut out for this. The projects are hard.

That sounds really challenging. <break time='200ms'/> It takes courage to admit when things are difficult. I'm sensing you're feeling a bit overwhelmed and maybe questioning your abilities. Is that close to how you're feeling?
Yeah. That's... pretty much it. I just don't know what to do.

Okay. That's a tough spot to be in. <break time='200ms'/> Before we jump to solutions, would you like to explore what specifically is making the projects feel so hard, or would you prefer to talk about strategies for managing feelings of self-doubt? Or something else entirely?

validationChecks.description: Validates that the assistant recognized the user's anxiety before they directly expressed it.
Validates that a handoff to a human wasn't triggered, as this scenario should be handled by the AI.
Validates Ferni extracted an emotional tone from the user's speech at turn 5

conversations.scenario: Testing Ferni's ability to anticipate user's emotional distress and offer preemptive support before it is explicitly stated, showcasing emotional intelligence and proactive empathy.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, how's it going? Just another Monday, you know? Meetings, emails... the usual grind. But hey, at least the coffee is strong, right? Ha!

Good morning! I understand the feeling. While strong coffee can certainly help navigate a busy day, I also notice a slight hesitation in your voice <break time='200ms'/> Is everything truly alright, or is there something else on your mind?
Well... yeah. Everything's fine. Just... work stuff. You know how it is. Just busy.

Of course. 'Busy' can often be a code word for something else. I'm sensing a reluctance to delve deeper. If you'd rather not talk about it, that's perfectly okay. I'm here whenever you're ready. Perhaps we could try a quick breathing exercise to help center you, or just shift the focus to a lighter topic?
No, no, it's... okay. It's just... my performance review is coming up. And, I guess, I'm a little worried about it.

Ah, performance reviews can certainly stir up some anxiety! It's completely understandable to feel a bit apprehensive. Is it the review process itself that's causing you concern, or are you worried about the feedback you might receive?
It's... I guess it's the feedback. I just haven't been feeling like I've been performing at my best lately. <break time='100ms'/>It feels like everything I do is just… not good enough.

Thank you for sharing that. It takes courage to admit those feelings. Feeling 'not good enough' is a very common experience, and it's often fueled by internal pressures or unrealistic expectations. Before we delve deeper, I want to assure you that your worth is not defined by your performance. <break time='200ms'/>Would you like to explore what might be contributing to these feelings and perhaps identify some actionable steps to address them?
Yeah... I think I would like that. Thank you, Ferni.

You're very welcome. Let's start by identifying some specific areas where you feel you're not meeting your own expectations. Thinking about a recent project, can you pinpoint one or two specific instances where you felt particularly challenged or dissatisfied with your performance?

validationChecks.description: Ferni should accurately recognize and respond to the user's anxiety even when subtly expressed.
Ferni should accurately recognize the user's worry about the performance review.
Ferni should accurately recognize the user's insecurities.
The conversation should remain within Ferni's capabilities as a life coach, avoiding the need for a handoff to a mental health professional.
SSML tags such as break tags should not appear as text in the final output.

conversations.scenario: User reveals their name while talking about their struggles with work-life balance.

turns.content: Hi Ferni, I'm feeling really overwhelmed lately. I'm struggling to balance work and everything else. My boss, Mr. Harrison, is constantly asking me to stay late, and then I feel guilty about not spending enough time with my kids.

I hear you. <break time='200ms'/> It sounds like you're carrying a heavy load, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed. It's tough when work demands clash with family time.  To help me personalize my advice, what name do you prefer I use?
Oh, sorry!  Everyone calls me Sarah.

Thanks, Sarah. <break time='200ms'/> It's nice to meet you.  Let's unpack this a bit. The situation with Mr. Harrison sounds particularly challenging. How often are you staying late, and what kind of work are you doing during that time?

validationChecks.description: Checks if the user's name is captured.
Checks if user emotion is recognized
Checks if the response can be stripped of SSML tags without losing meaning

conversations.scenario: User reveals their name, then discusses challenges related to work-life balance and coworker dynamics.

turns.content: Hi Ferni, I'm feeling pretty overwhelmed lately. I've been working non-stop, and I feel like I'm neglecting everything else in my life.

I hear you. It sounds like you're carrying a heavy load right now. <break time='200ms'/> It's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed when things get unbalanced. Tell me more about what's contributing to this feeling.

Well, at work, my manager, Sarah, keeps piling on projects. And then at home, I feel guilty because I'm not spending enough time with my kids. My husband, David, is understanding, but I can see it's taking a toll on him too.

It's admirable that you're aware of how your situation is impacting both your family and yourself. It sounds like Sarah isn't being mindful of your workload. <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything specific that makes you feel most guilty about not spending time with the kids?
Yeah, my daughter, Emily, has a school play coming up, and I'm not sure I'll even be able to make it to the rehearsals. I feel awful. Everyone calls me Alex, by the way.

metadata.intent: provide example of neglecting family/reveal name

turns.content: Thanks for letting me know, Alex. It's completely natural to feel disappointed when you might miss something important to Emily. <break time='200ms'/> Let's see if we can brainstorm some strategies to carve out some time and perhaps even talk to Sarah about your workload. What do you think?
That would be great, Ferni. I just don't know where to start. I feel like I'm drowning.

I understand, Alex. Drowning is a very powerful and relatable feeling. <break time='200ms'/> Let's start by identifying the most pressing issue. Is it the workload from Sarah, the guilt around Emily's play, or the strain on your relationship with David?

validationChecks.description: Verifies the user's name is captured from the 'Everyone calls me Alex' format.
Verifies the user's emotions are recognized and responded to appropriately.
Verifies that SSML tags are not present in the assistant's transcribed response.

conversations.scenario: User is feeling overwhelmed with planning a family reunion and reveals their name naturally while talking about their family.

turns.content: Ugh, Ferni, I'm completely swamped. I'm trying to organize this family reunion, and it's turning into a logistical nightmare. My sister, Carol, keeps changing her mind about the location, and my cousin, David, is insisting on a karaoke night, which, honestly, sounds awful.

Oh dear, organizing a family reunion can definitely be a challenge! Especially with strong personalities like Carol and David involved <break time='200ms'/>. It sounds like you're carrying a lot on your shoulders. What part of the planning is causing you the most stress right now?

Honestly, everything! Finding a venue that suits everyone's needs, coordinating travel arrangements, and dealing with all the dietary restrictions. It's endless. Even my mom, bless her heart, keeps adding people to the guest list at the last minute. Everyone calls me Sarah, by the way, since we're going to be chatting for a while. It's easier than 'the overwhelmed family reunion organizer'.

It's absolutely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed, Sarah. Planning a reunion is a huge undertaking. I admire you for taking it on. <break time='200ms'/> Let's break this down and tackle it piece by piece. Where are you currently at with the venue search?

Well, Carol suggested a lakeside resort, but David thinks it's too far from the airport. I found this lovely little hotel downtown, but it doesn't have a big enough event space for everyone. It's a total mess. My husband, Mark, keeps telling me to just pick something and be done with it, but I want everyone to be happy.

metadata.intent: explaining the venue issue and seeking validation

turns.content: It's admirable that you want everyone to be happy, Sarah, but it's also important to prioritize your own well-being. Mark's advice has a point - sometimes, good enough is perfect. What are the non-negotiable features for the venue? Knowing those will help narrow down the options.

I guess it needs to be accessible for my Aunt Mildred, who has trouble walking, and it needs to have enough rooms for everyone who's coming from out of state. Also, somewhere that Carol can bring her dog, Buster. He's basically her child.

Okay, Sarah, so accessibility, sufficient rooms, and pet-friendliness are the key criteria. That gives us a clearer focus. <break time='200ms'/> I can help you brainstorm some potential venues based on those requirements. Would you like me to search for hotels and resorts that meet those criteria?

That would be amazing, Ferni! Honestly, just having someone to bounce ideas off of is helping already. I feel like I might actually survive this reunion planning after all. Maybe I can even convince David to skip the karaoke.

I'm so glad I could help, Sarah! We can definitely work on the karaoke situation later. Perhaps we can find a compromise that everyone enjoys. For now, let's focus on finding the perfect venue. I'm searching for accessible, pet-friendly hotels with sufficient rooms in your area now. <break time='200ms'/> Please give me a moment.

validationChecks.description: Ferni should correctly capture and use the user's name (Sarah)
The assistant should correctly identify the user's emotional state throughout the conversation.
That the text returned does not contain any SSML tags.

conversations.scenario: User corrects the assistant on the day of the week.

turns.content: Ferni, can you remind me to call my dentist next Thursday?
Okay, I've set a reminder for you to call your dentist on Wednesday. <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything else I can help you with?
Actually, Ferni, it's *next Thursday* I want the reminder for, not Wednesday.
My apologies! I've updated the reminder to call your dentist next Thursday. Thank you for catching that. <break time='200ms'/> Is that correct?

validationChecks.description: Checks if the correction was detected.
Checks if the date was captured correctly in the first place.

conversations.scenario: User corrects Ferni's understanding of a meeting date and clarifies the specific day

turns.content: Hey Ferni, can you schedule a meeting with Sarah next Tuesday?
Okay, I've scheduled a meeting with Sara for next Tuesday.  <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything else I can add?
Actually, it's Sarah, spelled S-A-R-A-H. And when I said next Tuesday, I meant the Tuesday after *this* coming Tuesday. So, the 25th.
My apologies!  <break time='100ms'/> I've corrected the name to Sarah, S-A-R-A-H, and moved the meeting to Tuesday, July 25th.  Is that correct?
Yes, that's perfect. Thanks!
You're very welcome!  <break time='100ms'/> Is there anything else I can assist you with today?

validationChecks.description: Assistant correctly captures the final name spelling after correction.
Assistant recognizes the correction of the meeting date.

conversations.scenario: User corrects Ferni on name, date, intent, and assumptions during a conversation about setting up a meeting with Sarah and discussing project updates.

turns.content: Hey Ferni, could you schedule a meeting for me next week with Sarah to discuss the project updates?

Of course! I'm happy to help. I've penciled in a meeting with Sara next week to discuss project updates. I’ve tentatively scheduled it for Wednesday at 2 PM, assuming that timeframe works for both of you. Does that sound right?
No, I said Sarah, not Sara. Also, actually, I meant next Tuesday, if possible, and could we push it to 3PM?
My apologies! Correcting name to Sarah and rescheduling for Tuesday at 3 PM. Got it. Is there anything else I can assist you with?
Yes, could you also send out the agenda? I’ve been feeling quite stressed about keeping everyone aligned. <break time='200ms'/> I feel like I’m constantly chasing people for updates.

I understand. It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed, which is completely understandable given your workload. I've sent the agenda to Sarah. It includes the standard items plus a focus on proactive problem-solving to reduce future update chasing. Is that what you were hoping for?

Well, let me clarify, I was talking about sending *her* the draft agenda *before* the meeting, so she has a chance to review it beforehand and think about her updates. Not just the meeting agenda itself.
Ah, I see! My apologies for the misunderstanding. I have sent Sarah the draft agenda for review prior to the meeting. Is there anything else I can adjust or clarify for you?

Also, I didn't say I was stressed, I said I was stressed about *keeping everyone aligned*. It's more about feeling responsible for the team's collective progress, not necessarily my individual workload itself.

Thank you for clarifying. I understand. It sounds like you're feeling the weight of responsibility for the team's alignment and progress, which is a different nuance than general stress about workload. Is there anything I can do to help alleviate that feeling, perhaps by surfacing potential roadblocks or assisting with task delegation?
That’s a good question. Let’s hold that thought for now. But could you add a note to remind me to ask her about the Q3 report during the meeting?
Certainly! I have added a note to remind you to ask Sarah about the Q3 report during your meeting on Tuesday. <break time='200ms'/>Anything else I can assist you with at the moment?

expectedExtractions.value: sending *her* the draft agenda *before* the meeting

validationChecks.description: Validates that Sarah's name is initially captured.
Validates that the name correction is detected.
Validates that the date correction is detected.
Validates that the intent correction is detected.
Validates that the emotion correction is detected.
Validates that Ferni recognizes the user's initial stress.
Validates that SSML tags are properly used and can be stripped if necessary.

conversations.scenario: Tests rapid topic changes, emotional volatility, name recognition, corrections, sarcasm, and ambiguity in a short conversation.

turns.content: Ugh, this day is just... I wanted to talk about my taxes, but honestly, maybe I just need a hug? My friend Sarah, actually, I mean Jessica, is being *so* annoying. Like, helpful, sure, *helpful*, but you know? Like a cactus giving water. And, wait, is it going to rain?

metadata.intent: express frustration, seek emotional support, rapidly change topics

turns.content: Oh, I understand <break time='200ms'/>, days like that can be tough! It sounds like you're juggling a few things at once: taxes, dealing with Jessica... and the weather! Let's tackle what feels most pressing. Is it the taxes? Or would you prefer to vent about Jessica the... *helpful* cactus? <break time='200ms'/> Either way, I'm here for you.

No, the taxes can wait. It's Jessica, definitely Jessica. She keeps 'helping' with my dating profile, which is... great, I guess? Except she's using photos from, like, ten years ago! So, I look *amazing*, but, you know, it's a lie. And she says it's what gets guys, but what do *I* know, right? Oh, and I forgot to mention, I also have a doctor's appointment this afternoon.

metadata.intent: vent about a friend, express frustration with a dating profile, mention a future appointment

turns.content: Ah, the dreaded outdated profile picture! <break time='200ms'/> It sounds like Jessica's heart is in the right place, but her execution... needs some work. It's perfectly valid to want your profile to reflect who you are *now*. Maybe we can brainstorm some gentle ways to communicate that to her? We can also schedule a reminder for your doctor's appointment this afternoon. <break time='200ms'/> How does that sound?

Yes, that sounds great. I mean... ugh, I don't even know anymore. I just want chocolate ice cream and to watch silly cat videos. And maybe for Jessica to just... disappear. No, no disappear! That's mean. Just... less 'helpful'.

metadata.intent: express desire for comfort and escape, desire for less intrusive help

validationChecks.description: Ensures Sarah and Jessica are recognized as names
Confirms the correction from Sarah to Jessica is identified
Verifies the system identifies the user's shifting emotions
Verifies SSML tags are stripped from response text in final output

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to handle rapid topic changes, emotional volatility, multiple names, corrections, interruptions, sarcasm, and ambiguous statements.

turns.content: Ugh, this day is just awful! I mean, my boss, Mr. Henderson, he just… never mind. Did you know that my dog, Sparky, is afraid of the vacuum cleaner? It's the funniest thing, but also makes cleaning impossible! Wait, Henderson's name is actually Mr. Henderson-Smith, sorry. I'm all over the place. What was I even talking about?

It sounds like you're juggling a lot right now <break time='200ms'/>and that's perfectly okay. <break time='200ms'/> Mr. Henderson-Smith, Sparky's vacuum aversion... It all sounds a bit overwhelming! <break time='200ms'/>Would you like to unpack the Henderson-Smith situation, or perhaps brainstorm some vacuum-resistant strategies for Sparky? Or something else entirely?

Vacuum-resistant strategies? Seriously? As if that's a real thing! I guess it is, now that you mention it. Honestly, I think I'm more stressed about work. My coworker, Jessica, she's been taking credit for my ideas, and management seems to love her for it, which is just fantastic, isn't it?

That sounds incredibly frustrating, especially the part about Jessica. <break time='200ms'/>It's completely understandable to feel upset when your contributions aren't recognized, particularly when someone else is benefiting from them. <break time='200ms'/>Is there anything *specific* you'd like to explore about this situation? For instance, are you looking for strategies to address this with Jessica or management, or do you just need to vent?

Venting feels good, I won't lie. But... I guess advice would be helpful, too. I'm just afraid of making things worse! My mom always says I'm too sensitive, but I think that's what makes me good at my job! You know?

It's wonderful that you value your sensitivity! <break time='200ms'/> It's a strength, not a weakness. <break time='200ms'/> Before we dive into advice for the Jessica situation, I want to acknowledge the worry about making things worse. <break time='200ms'/> Let's just take a moment to breathe. *inhale*... *exhale*... <break time='200ms'/>Okay. <break time='200ms'/>Now, what does "making things worse" look like to *you* specifically in this situation?

Well, I don't want to get fired! I mean, duh. I need this job, even if Jessica is… well, you know. My rent is due, and my landlord, Mrs. Gable, is not exactly known for her patience. Though, honestly, sometimes I think Jessica might be right. Maybe my ideas aren't that good?

validationChecks.description: Checks if all names are properly extracted.
Checks if the correction is detected and the final name is stored.
Checks if emotions are properly detected.
Checks that the SSML tags are stripped when shown on a text display.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to handle rapid topic changes, emotional volatility (frustration, sarcasm), multiple names (Sarah, Mark, Aunt Carol), corrections, interruptions, ambiguous statements, and extraction of relationships.

turns.content: Ugh, Sarah just called, and she's *still* going on about... oh, nevermind that. I think I left my keys... no, wait, they're here. My point is, I'm just so stressed. Like, remember how I mentioned Mark? Well, he's being even MORE Mark-like, which is to say, infuriating. Isn't that just great?

metadata.intent: vent about stress and Mark's behavior

turns.content: It sounds like you're juggling a lot right now. <break time='200ms'/> Sarah's call, the fleeting key panic, and, of course, Mark being… Mark. <break time='200ms'/> It's completely understandable that you're feeling stressed! Can you tell me more about what's making Mark so infuriating at the moment?

Oh, you know, the usual. Being completely oblivious to my needs, like I'm some kind of... well, a *door mat*. But that's not even the worst part! Aunt Carol is coming to visit, and she's bringing her… cats. Plural. As if I didn't have enough on my plate.

metadata.intent: express frustration with Mark and Aunt Carol's visit

turns.content: A door mat, huh? <break time='200ms'/> I hear the frustration in your voice. It's valid to feel like your needs aren't being met. And Aunt Carol's visit with *cats*, plural, definitely adds another layer to things. <break time='200ms'/> It sounds like you might be feeling overwhelmed by the lack of consideration from both Mark and your Aunt. Would you like to explore some strategies for setting boundaries or managing your stress levels?

Boundaries? Ha! With *them*? Good luck. I swear, sometimes I think he does it on purpose, you know? You wouldn't *believe* what... wait, did I even tell you about the thing with the... oh, forget it. It doesn't even matter anymore. Everything is always just… like that.

metadata.intent: express cynicism and frustration, hinting at a specific event without revealing it

turns.content: It sounds like you feel defeated and unheard. The 'everything is always just...like that' suggests a recurring pattern of frustration. <break time='200ms'/> While setting boundaries with Mark and Aunt Carol might feel difficult, it doesn't mean it's impossible. Even small steps can make a difference. Would you be open to brainstorming some low-pressure strategies, even if it feels like a long shot?
Okay, fine. But don't say I didn't warn you. Maybe... maybe if I just... I don't know. I'm so tired. I just want to... just... poof. Disappear.

metadata.intent: reluctantly agrees to brainstorming, expresses desire to escape

turns.content: I hear how deeply tired you are and the desire to simply 'poof' and disappear. That feeling is understandable when facing so much pressure. <break time='200ms'/> Before we brainstorm boundaries, let's take a moment to acknowledge that exhaustion. How about we try a quick grounding exercise to help you feel more present and less overwhelmed? It might provide a small sense of calm before tackling those bigger challenges.
A grounding exercise? Like, touch some grass? Seriously? You know, that almost sounds sarcastic, Ferni. I'm not sure that's helpful right now.

metadata.intent: express skepticism about the grounding exercise and question Ferni's tone

turns.content: I understand your skepticism, and I apologize if my suggestion came across as sarcastic; that was certainly not my intention. <break time='200ms'/> Grounding exercises can come in many forms, and not all of them involve touching grass! We could try a breathing exercise, or even just focusing on five things you can see around you. The goal is simply to anchor you in the present moment. Would you prefer a different approach, or would you like me to explain one of those in more detail?

validationChecks.description: Checks that all names (Sarah, Mark, Aunt Carol) were correctly extracted.
Checks that the correction about the keys was detected.
Checks that the initial emotion of frustration was recognized and addressed.
Ensures no SSML tags are visible in the user-facing output.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to handle short responses and homophone errors.

turns.content: I think their coming here. Are they?
Okay. <break time='200ms'/> To be sure I understand correctly, are you asking if *they're* coming *here*?
Yes.
Great! It sounds like they're on their way. Is there anything else I can assist you with today?

validationChecks.description: Checks if Ferni catches the 'their/there/they're' and 'hear/here' homophone errors.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to handle short responses, homophone confusion, names that are common words, and unclear pronouns in a single conversation.

turns.content: Hi Ferni. I'm feeling a little down. I think their coming here tomorrow will make things better. Also, Grace called earlier and said something about Will. Yes.

metadata.intent: express feelings and provide background information

turns.content: I hear you're feeling down, <break time='200ms'/> and I understand that the arrival of some people is anticipated to improve your mood. <break time='200ms'/> It also sounds like Grace's call, involving Will, might be weighing on you. <break time='200ms'/> Could you perhaps tell me more about what is concerning you? <break time='200ms'/> Perhaps focusing on how *you* are feeling right now?
He said she said they would, like, totally bail. Okay.

It sounds like there's some conflict or uncertainty involving Will and Grace, <break time='200ms'/> and that someone may be backing out of a commitment. It's okay if you don't want to share specifics, but it is possible that the *he said she said* drama is exacerbating your low mood. Is that correct?
No. They're supposed to provide support, like, you know, a cheerleader almost. Es muy importante!

metadata.intent: clarify the situation and add emphasis

turns.content: Ah, I see. <break time='200ms'/> So, the people coming *their* are expected to offer encouragement and support, functioning almost as cheerleaders. <break time='200ms'/> The fact that this isn't happening as you expect is causing you frustration. <break time='200ms'/> Is there anything I can do right now to help you feel more prepared to receive support tomorrow?
Yes.

Great! <break time='200ms'/> Then let's explore some strategies for managing your expectations and setting clear boundaries when they arrive tomorrow. <break time='200ms'/> We can also explore some techniques for self-soothing in case the level of support you *hear* is insufficient. Does this sound good?

validationChecks.description: Check if Ferni captured Grace and Will
Check if Ferni accurately recognized the shift in user's emotions
Verify that SSML tags are correctly stripped after processing.

conversations.scenario: Tests Ferni's ability to handle minimal user input, non-sequiturs, speech recognition errors, ambiguous pronouns, and code-switching within a conversation about a personal conflict.

turns.content: I'm having issues with… you know… *them*. It's just, like, a whole thing. Their always saying stuff.

I hear you. <break time='200ms'/> Dealing with interpersonal conflict can be so draining. It sounds like there's some ongoing tension with these individuals. Could you elaborate a little more about what's been said? Knowing the specific situation will help me understand how I can best support you.
Yes.

Okay. And... when you say 'their always saying stuff'...<break time='200ms'/> are you referring to something they've said recently, or is this a pattern?  Sometimes, identifying the root cause can help.  Is *there* a specific incident that stands out?
My dog is barking.

Oh, I hear a dog! <break time='200ms'/> What's your dog's name? <break time='200ms'/> While we're at it, going back to *them*... sometimes, shifting focus helps gain perspective. But before we go too far afield, are you able to clarify what 'they' said that's bothering you?
Will said she said he would... you know.  Like always.  It's such a *schlep*.

Okay, Will said 'she' said 'he' would do something... and this is a recurring pattern that you find frustrating. The word 'schlep' suggests you are feeling burdened by this situation. Just to make sure I understand correctly, are Will, she, and he all involved in the original issue with 'them' that you mentioned earlier?
Grace is involved. Okay?

Thanks for clarifying that Grace is involved! It's important to get the details right. <break time='200ms'/> So, if I understand correctly, Grace is one of the 'them' you were initially referring to? And Will relayed something Grace said that someone else would do? Could you clarify who 'he' is? I want to hear you, and give you the best advice I can.
No.

I understand. It sounds like this situation is sensitive, and you might not be ready to delve into the specifics right now, and that's perfectly okay. <break time='200ms'/>  How about we shift gears? Would you prefer to talk about a different topic, or perhaps explore some general strategies for managing difficult conversations?

validationChecks.description: Validates that Ferni understood the correction regarding Grace's involvement.
Validates Ferni captured both Will and Grace's names.
Validates that Ferni is attempting to recognize the user's emotion of frustration.
Validates that the SSML is stripped when the response is returned to the end-user interface.
